notes: Who, then, is the beast from the sea? Following are some clues to its identity: 1. The beast has political power-from it stem many kings (13:1; 17:12). 2. It stands in blatant opposition to God, representing blasphemy with no shame (13:1). 3. It receives its power and authority from Satan himself (13:2). 4. One of its heads receives a deadly wound by a sword and is healed (13:3, 14). 5. The whole world knows about the healing and is amazed (13:3). 6. The people of the world worship the beast (13:4, 8). 7. The world marvels at the power of the beast (13:4). 8. The beast is able to speak great things, including blasphemies (13:5, 6). 9. It has power for forty-two months (13:5). 10. It has power to make war with and overcome the Saints (13:7). 11. It has power over all nations (13:7). 12. A second beast has power to give life to an image of the first beast, enabling it even to speak (13:15). 13. The second beast causes all who do not worship the first beast to be put to death (13:15).

Hebrew traditions describe an ancient sea-beast named Leviathan (sometimes called Rahab) and a earth-beast named Behemoth. According to some of the stories, Leviathan was "subdued by Yahweh at the dawn of creation (Ps 74: 14; Isa. 27: 1). In apocalyptic literature (II Esd. 6: 52; II Bar. 29: 3-8), Leviathan is represented as destined to break loose from his bonds at the end of the present era, only to suffer a second and final defeat." [Interpreter's Dictionary of the Bible (K-Q): 116.] Behemoth is usually referred to as "the male counterpart of Leviathan, which lives on land, while its mate lives in the sea (II Bar. 29: 4; II Esd. 6: 49, 52)…" This beast, like Leviathan, will challenge God during the last days, but will suffer defeat. According to the tradition, "his carcass will then be served as food to the righteous." [Interpreter's Dictionary of the Bible (A-D): 376.]

Otho = 8 months leaders over Rome for a short period. Vitellus = 8 months Vespasian A.D. 69 -- A.D. 79 Titus A.D. 79 -- A.D. 81 Domitian A.D. 81 -- A.D. 96 (Revelation is written) (Reed C. Durham, "Revelations: The Plainest Book Ever Written," New Era, May 1973) The theory has many variations and is speculative, but it does seem to fit historically, as well as scripturally. What is most important to John is that we understand that Satan has power over the kingdoms of this world. He has sworn to use this power to war against the saints of God. The numbers that he used may have more symbolic meaning than predictive value. An interpretation of them is given later in these notes.

One Latter-day Saint advocate of the theory is Reed Durham. In his New Era article of May 1973, he lists the Roman Emperors that may fit the description of seven and ten. Augustus 27 B.C. -- A.D. 14 (Christ Born) Tiberius A.D. 14 -- A.D. 37 (Christ's ministry and death) Caligula A.D. 37 -- A.D. 41 Claudius A.D. 41 -- A.D. 54 Nero A.D. 54 -- A.D. 68 (Nero Redivivus myth) Galba = 6 months Three military generals who acted as

quotes.text: When God made use of the figure of a beast in visions to the prophets He did it to represent those kingdoms which had degenerated and become corrupt, savage and beast-like in their dispositions, even the degenerate kingdoms of the wicked world. . . . The beast that rose up out of the sea should be translated the image of a beast"-meaning that it was symbolic rather than literal.
quotes.speaker: Joseph Smith
quotes.attr: Joseph Smith, History of the Church, 5:341, 345

quotes.text: Note that the beasts of Satan are in contrast (symbolically) to the witnesses of the Lord in chapter eleven. Both pairs act under their respective authority, and perform mighty miracles. Keep in mind, the two beasts "represent kingdoms" according to the JST. Kiddle and Ross, in their Revelation of St. John, list some points of comparison (pp. 256-261): Two Prophets (Chapter 11) The Land Beast (13:11ff) 2 witnesses are 2 prophets Land beast is called the false prophet (13:14; 19:20: 20:10) they are leading men to Christ beast is leading men to worship false gods they perform great miracles beast performs great miracles they stand before God (11:4) he exercise authority before the 1st beast they have special power over fire he makes fire come down from heaven receive breath of life from God animates the image with the breath of life they convince men of God's power 11:11) kills all that don't worship the image (13:15) 2 lampstands & 2 olive trees Has 2 horns of a lamb
quotes.attr: taken from JM Ford, Revelation, Anchor Bible Series, 38:224

quotes.text: A popular theory is that the seven heads and ten horns represent, at least in John's time, the emperors of Rome. William Barclay summarizes the theory in the following words: "This beast has seven heads and ten horns. These stand for the rulers and the emperors of Rome. Since Augustus, the first Roman Emperor, there had been seven emperors; Tiberius, A.D. 14 - 37; Caligula, A.D. 37-41; Claudius, A.D. 41-54; Nero, A.D. 55-68; Vespasian, A.D. 69-79; Titus, A.D. 79-81; Domitian, A.D. 81-96. These seven emperors are the seven heads of the beast. But in addition it is said that the beast had ten horns. The explanation of this second figure lies in this. After the death of Nero there was a short period of almost complete chaos. In eighteen months three different men briefly occupied the imperial power, Galba, Otho and Vitellius. They are not included in John's list of the seven heads but are included in the list of the ten horns.
quotes.speaker: William Barclay
quotes.attr: William Barclay, The Revelation of John, Vol. 2, pp. 88-89

quotes.text: The monster which John now sees emerging out of the sea is the same as the one he has already described as rising from the abyss (11: 7). In the Greek Old Testament abyssos is used as a rendering for the Great Deep, the primaeval ocean of the creation story (Gen. 1: 2; 7: 11), and for the sea in general (Job 28: 14; 38: 16). It is said of Wisdom that she guided Israel through the Red Sea, but engulfed their enemies 'and cast them up again out of the depths of the abyss' (Wis. 10: 19). In Romans 10: 7 Paul quotes a Greek version of Deuteronomy 30: 13, in which the question, 'Who will cross the sea for us?' has become 'Who will descend into the abyss?'; and he takes the abyss to be synonymous with the underworld. The sea, then is the nether reservoir of evil, which has its upper counterpart in the heavenly sea of glass.
quotes.speaker: G. B. Caird
quotes.source: A Commentary on the Revelation of St. John the Divine
quotes.attr: G. B. Caird, A Commentary on the Revelation of St. John the Divine, p. 161

quotes.text: The names of blasphemy upon the seven heads may reflect the tendency of the Roman emperors to assume titles of deity. Mounce explains that, "During his lifetime Augustus had allowed his eastern subjects to pay him divine honors, and at his death the Romans proclaimed him divus (one like the gods). On his coins Nero was referred to as Savior of the World. The Roman senate regularly declared its deceased emperors divine. Domitian was addressed as Dominus et Deus noster, Our Lord and God. To all who held a high view of God and his sovereignty such pretentious claims were sheer blasphemy.
quotes.speaker: Robert Mounce
quotes.attr: Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, p. 250

quotes.text: Recall that in apocalyptic the number seven carries the idea of completeness, and so does the number ten. Seven connotes completeness more in the form of perfection, while ten more in the form of breadth. This may be complete evil, both qualitatively and quantitatively. "A seven-headed beast would be an appropriate symbol for the ultimate enemy of the believing church.
quotes.speaker: Robert Mounce
quotes.attr: Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New International Commentary, p. 250

notes: Presented as the antithesis of the Lamb which was presented "as though it had been slain" (Rev. 5: 6); this beast had a head which was "as it were wounded to death." In Chapter 17 this beast is even introduced as one which "was and is not and is to come" (17: 8). This is an obvious parody of the name
Elder Mc Conkie explains that, "The devil gives power, positions of influence, and great authority to those kingdoms which follow him." [DNTC 3: 521.]

quotes.text: The translators have used the term "dragon" for "Devil." . . . There is a mistranslation of the word "dragon" in the second verse. The original word signifies the Devil and not "dragon" as translated. Read chapter 12 verse 9; it there reads, "that old serpent, called the Devil." And it ought to be translated "Devil" in this case, and not "dragon.
quotes.speaker: Joseph Smith
quotes.attr: Joseph Smith, Discourse of 8 April 1843, recorded by William Clayton; WJS, 186-87

quotes.text: First beast out of sea (Mediterranean Sea) is Rome and the Roman Empire with all of its parts. Leopard = Babylon; bear = Media; lion = Persia.
quotes.speaker: Reed C. Durham
quotes.source: Revelation: The Plainest Book Ever Written
quotes.attr: Reed C. Durham, "Revelation: The Plainest Book Ever Written," New Era, May 1973, 26

notes: "Some spiritualizers say the beast that received the wound was Nebuchadnezzar, some Constantine, some Mohammed, and others the Roman Catholic Church; but we will look at what John saw in relation to this beast. Now for the wasp's nest. The translators have used the term 'dragon' for devil. Now it was a beast that John saw in heaven, and he was then speaking of 'things which must shortly come to pass;' and consequently the beast that John saw could not be Nebuchadnezzar. The beast John saw was an actual beast, and an actual intelligent being gives him his power, and his seat, and great authority. It was not to represent a beast in heaven: it was an angel in heaven who has power in the last days to do a work. "'All the world wondered after the beast,' Nebuchadnezzar and Constantine the Great not excepted. And if the beast was all the world, how could the world wonder after the beast? It must have been a wonderful beast to cause all human beings to wonder after it; and I will venture to say that when God allows the old devil to give power to the beast to destroy the inhabitants of the earth, all will wonder." [Joseph Smith, Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, p. 293.]
were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed This could be many different evil kingdoms, ideologies in the last days. Communism has come down but will it be revived again?

quotes.text: Presented as the antithesis of the Lamb which was presented "as though it had been slain" (Rev. 5: 6); this beast had a head which was "as it were wounded to death." In Chapter 17 this beast is even introduced as one which "was and is not and is to come" (17: 8). This is an obvious parody of the name of God. (See Ex. 3: 14 and Revelation 1: 18.) Again, we look to history for a possible typological insight to the meaning of the strange language of verse three. History does not provide the answer to how the verse will be fulfilled, but it does provide clues as to its possible interpretation. "Many commentators take the slaughtered head as standing for one of the Roman emperors. Caligula is a likely candidate in that he was taken with a serious illness and recovered (Suetonius, Calig. 14; Dio Cassius lix.8). His attempt to set up his statue in the temple could give rise to verse 6 ('He opened his mouth for blasphemies against God… and his tabernacle') and the many altars erected to him throughout the empire (Josephus, Ant. xviii.8.1) would encourage the kind of universal worship reflected in verse 8 ('All that dwell on earth shall worship him'). Most writers, however, who take the slain head as a Roman emperor choose Nero. This tyrant, who gained the throne by the treachery of a scheming mother (banished by Caligula but brought back by her uncle, Claudius, whom she married and later poisoned), instituted in AD 64 a savage persecution of the Christian church. In order to escape the ignominious death prescribed for a public enemy, he took his own life in June of 68. So evil was he that many (especially in the eastern provinces) thought his death in a private villa outside Rome could not possibly be true. Although he was given a public funeral, the rumor persisted for a number of years that he had not died but had gone to Parthia where he remained in hiding to return some day at the head of a mighty army to regain his lost dominion. During the next two decades several pretenders arose claiming to be Nero (Tacitus, Hist. i.78; ii.8; Suetonius, Nero 57), two of whom were well received in Parthia. By the end of the century the belief that he was still alive had faded. It was replaced with the expectation that he would rise from the dead and return to seize power.
quotes.speaker: Robert Mounce
quotes.attr: Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, p. 252

quotes.text: Nero Redivivus This was the first persecution of Christians conducted by the Roman imperial government and with it, the name of Nero would be indelibly remembered for being the evil man he was. So cruel and so much a tyrant was he that people rejoiced at the news of his suicidal death. Suetonius said that "such was the public rejoicing that the people put on liberty-caps and ran about all over the city." 12 And yet many people, as with Hitler's death, could not bring themselves to believe this tyrant was really gone. In fact, some people believed he would surely come again with even increased power to inflict domination and cruelty upon mankind. This belief was commonly known in Christian historical sources as the "Nero Redivivus" myth. Suetonius said "there were some who for a long time decorated his tomb with spring and summer flowers, and now produced his statues on the rostra in the fringed toga, and now his edicts, as if he were still alive and would shortly return and deal destruction to his enemies. 13 It even appears possible that John, in writing the Revelation may have used this popular myth about Nero. However, it is certain that neither John nor his readers could have seriously entertained a belief that Nero would literally [page 25] be revived to life again (the Christians knew he would be resurrected with all evilness at the second resurrection), but figuratively they might have believed Nero could live again in the life and deeds of another person. (Reed C. Durham, "Revelation: The Plainest Book Ever Written," New Era, May 1973, 24-25) Nero-like, Domitian also became obsessed in the belief that he was divine; but Domitian, unlike Nero, took a further step in this obsession. Domitian was the first Roman emperor who commanded that he be worshiped as a god. This even out-Neroed Nero. And to insure that he received the proper homage as deity, he inaugurated "emperor worship" along with the old Roman priestcraft and cultus, and he revitalized the imperial priestcraft and empowered it to enforce this worship.
quotes.speaker: Reed C. Durham
quotes.source: Revelation: The Plainest Book Ever Written
quotes.attr: Reed C. Durham, "Revelation: The Plainest Book Ever Written," New Era, May 1973, 25

notes: "Some say it means the kingdom of the world. One thing is sure, it does not mean the kingdom of the Saints. Suppose we admit that it means the kingdoms of the world, what propriety would there be in saying, Who is able to make war with my great big self? If these spiritualized interpretations are true, the book contradicts itself in almost every verse. But they are not true." [Joseph Smith, Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, p. 293.] This statement from the Prophet caused Elder Mc Conkie to write that "the beast does not represent all the world, but only selected 'kingdoms of the earth.'" [Bruce R. Mc Conkie, DNTC 3: 521.]
How does one worship the devil? Think of the opposite situation, how does one worship the Lord? Obviously, by keeping the commandments and in emulating Him.

quotes.text: Who is like unto the beast? The people of the world offer to the beast praise and admiration that should be reserved for God. In Exodus, for example, the people exclaim, "Who is like unto thee, O Lord, among the gods? who is like unto thee . . .?" (Ex. 15:11; Ps. 89:8; Isa. 44:7; Micah 7:18).
quotes.source: Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co.
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 168

notes: speaking great things and blasphemies a) Articulate with the Language such as Nehor, Korohor, etc b) Heresies and false Doctrine

quotes.text: We have already discussed the significance of 42 months. As the length of time that Antiochus Epiphanes executed his terrible reign and dire persecution over the Jews (168 B.C. to 156 B.C.), it "became symbolic of a period of terror and demonic evil before the final victory of God.
quotes.speaker: J. M. Ford
quotes.attr: J. M. Ford, Revelation, The Anchor Bible Series, Vol. 38, p. 222

notes: This activity is described elsewhere by Paul. In 2 Thess. 2:4, the man of sin is revealed as the son of perdition; "who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped; so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God."
blaspheme (blasphemeo) to speak evil; to speak reproachfully, rail at, revile

quotes.text: The monster is not Satan but the result of the unchecked work of Satan within institutions, communities, and countries. Wherever men refuse to be controlled by God or governed by truth, Satan rules; this is the danger of drive without humility. All spiritual aberrations are rooted here and are a result of the greatest of moral evils-pride. President Benson has warned the Saints against pride. "The central feature of pride," he instructs, "is enmity-enmity toward God and enmity toward our fellowmen. Enmity means 'hatred toward, hostility to, or a state of opposition.' It is the power by which Satan wishes to reign over us." He goes on to state that "the proud cannot accept the authority of God giving direction to their lives. (See Hel. 12:6.) They pit their perceptions of truth against God's great knowledge, their abilities versus God's priesthood power, their accomplishments against His mighty works." The prophet further states that the proud man gets no joy out of having anything but only out of having more than others. [Ezra Taft Benson, "Beware of Pride," Ensign, May 1989,4-7.] Thus, pride is competitive. It drives men on and on, never allowing satisfaction, all the while twisting their minds and souls into eternal selfishness. "I am the greatest," such insist, "therefore, what I want should happen. I'll step on anyone or anything that will not bow to my will." Sooner or later, such souls become saturated with the same poison that turned the son of the morning into the devil himself. Affected by the bite of the serpent from the sea, they carry his mark always with them. It affects both their labor (the mark on the hand) and their thought (the mark on the forehead). To these prideful, perverted souls, all men must eventually become slaves. Those who refuse are considered the enemy and soon become the persecuted. Since God will not submit to their program, will not follow their agenda, he becomes their ultimate enemy.
quotes.speaker: Richard D. Draper
quotes.source: Opening the Seven Seals: The Visions of John the Revelator [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co.
quotes.attr: Richard D. Draper, Opening the Seven Seals: The Visions of John the Revelator [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1991], 151-152

quotes.text: In the last days' (Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, p. 293), the beast shall have power over every nation, and shall make war with the saints and prevail over all except those who are true and faithful to the standards of the restored gospel.
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Doctrinal New Testament Commentary 3: 522

notes: Offerings were burned on the altars of Israel throughout Old Testament times in symbolic anticipation of the great sacrifice of Him who was called the “Lamb slain from the foundation of the world” (Revelation
Could this be the Hellenization of Christianity (1 Ne 13) and that later you have a typological church of the Devil (1 Ne 14)?
Cross reference with Jeremiah 15:2; Matt. 26:52 and Revelation 14:12-13

quotes.text: Here is the patience and the faith of the saints. Other translations (NIV, JB, RSV, NEB) clarify this phrase to indicate that the trials and persecution the Saints face will require much patience and faith if they hope to endure. Still another reading is the Saints know that even though some of them will suffer persecutions from those who worship the beast, in the end the Lord will execute his righteous judgments against the wicked.
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation, Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998, 170

quotes.text: John sees a second beast, which has the same power as the beast that preceded it. The second beast appears to be like Christ (the lamb), but he really is like Satan, his master (the dragon). This beast also is a symbol of one or more kings or kingdoms of the earth. [History of the Church, 5:341.] The second beast causes the people of the world to worship (meaning, perhaps, to follow or to give allegiance to) the first beast. He performs great miracles to deceive people, he gives life to the image of the first beast, and he kills many who will not worship that image. This beast (or kingdom) also controls the economy of the earth (buying and selling) and requires people to associate themselves with the beast if they wish to have part in that economy. This beast truly is an anti-Christ: he pretends to be a lamb, he performs great miracles (including fire from heaven, which all men see), he brings an image of the first beast to life, he requires the people of the world to worship that image instead of God, he takes away the exercise of agency of the people of the world. This second beast is referred to as a "false prophet" later in Revelation (16:13; 19:20; 20:10). Perhaps this beast, as a "prophet," represents religious philosophy and power, rather than the more political power of the first beast. Or perhaps he represents Satan's sophisticated propaganda machine, designed to flood the world with lies and false political and religious doctrines. Like his counterpart, he will remain in power until the Lord comes (19:19-20). His exact identity—whether as a kingdom or a false prophet—has not been revealed.
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation, Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998, 170

quotes.text: The second beast represents a local authority concerned with the worship of the first beast, probably the priesthood of the imperial cult, the most important cult in the province of Asia. The foundation of the provincial cult of Domitian at Ephesus late in the first century (involving the obligatory participation of the entire province of Asia) provided a climate in which enormous pressures were placed on Christians to be loyal citizens and participate in the imperial cult. Sacrifices and prayers were not ordinarily directed to the emperor but to the gods on his behalf, yet Christians generally refused to make any kind of sacrifices. Emperors were not officially regarded as gods until they were posthumously pronounced divus (Lat., 'divine') by the Roman senate, though Gaius (A.D. 37-41) and Domitian (A.D. 81-96) are exceptions. In the eastern provinces, however, they were often accorded cults while living.
quotes.speaker: James L. Mays
quotes.attr: James L. Mays, ed., Harper's Bible Commentary, p. 1314

quotes.text: All that Elder Mc Conkie will offer, and wisely so, on the identity of the Earth beast is, "Other evil and devil-led powers arise to exercise dominion over man in his fallen and spiritually degenerate state. The identity of these powers remains to be revealed.
quotes.attr: Doctrinal New Testament Commentary 3: 523

notes: This Earth Beast has all the power of the first beast b) Has power that forces the earth to worship the SEA-BEAST
This seems to imitate the Holy Ghost who serves to bear witness of Christ and the Father?

quotes.text: We have seen the priests of Pharaoh perform false miracles by the power of the devil. Among the Nephites in their darkest hour there were sorceries, and witchcrafts, and magics and the power of the evil one was wrought upon all the face of the land. (Mormon 1:19.) Witchcraft and sorcery and false miracles are not things of the past. Today also there are mediums and wizards who chirp and mutter as they arrange for their devotees to hear from the dead. Today also there are those who pretend to engage in great healing ministries. And what we now see is only the beginning. As the hour of the Second Coming draws nearer, and Satan gains greater power over more of his followers, we shall see even greater outpourings of evil power. Indeed, the day is not far distant when the evil one will make.
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Millennial Messiah, p. 83

notes: Our own day faces similar dangers concerning the worshipping of images. Though we are seldom forced at the point of the sword, many bow down to false images. Our trial seems to be rejecting images of secularism, nationalism, materialism, militarism, communism, etc. In a way, these are kingdoms that demand loyalty and obedience.

The deception of the people of the world is a commentary on their spiritual state. Satan cannot deceive a person against that person's will-belief in the lies of Satan is always accompanied by choosing sin or

quotes.text: The actual making of an image for the monster is a direct infringement of Exod. 20: 3-4 and possibly recalls the golden calf in Exod. 32. But the danger was ever present. The mad Caligula issued a decree commanding his statue to be adored. Tacitus (Annals 15: 29) records obeisance to the Roman emperor Nero after the defeat of Tiridades by Corbulo. The Parthian and Roman armies were gathered together with their standards and the images of their gods and between the two armies was a tribunal supporting a curule chair upon which was an effigy of Nero. The sacrifices were offered until Tiridades took the diadem off his head and laid it at the feet of the effigy. The present text may suggest something comparable. There were also numerous representations of the emperor on shields, coins, and standards, which were offensive to Jews.
quotes.speaker: J. M. Ford
quotes.attr: J. M. Ford, Revelation, The Anchor Bible Series, Vol. 38, pp. 224-5

quotes.text: The Great Imitator is able to blind the eyes and deceive the hearts of men and to put his own seal of verity, that of false miracles, on his damning philosophies. Thus those who place themselves wholly at his disposal have power to imitate the deeds of the prophets, as the magicians of Egypt imitated the miracles of Moses and as Simon the sorcerer sought to duplicate the works of Peter.
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Doctrinal New Testament Commentary, 3:524

notes: As mentioned earlier in these notes, the beast is not to be understood as the "Great and Abominable Church." The "Great and Abominable Church" is the Harlot who rides upon the beast in chapter 17. Often saints get these two confused. The mark of the beast does not identify the harlot, but those who worship the beast. Of course, both the beast and the harlot belong to Satan's kingdom.

foreheads." This is when the 144,000 are sealed. Soon thereafter, John mentions the "great multitude which no man could number of all nations" which are also sealed. Joseph Smith said that this "signifies sealing the blessing upon their heads, meaning the everlasting covenant, thereby making their calling and election sure." (TPJS, 321.) Since seals and names denote ownership, these high priests (D&C 77: 11) belong to Christ. They therefore bear his mark or brand and they hold his authority or priesthood. Even prior to Saints "making their calling and election sure" they receive Christ's "image upon their countenance" by experiencing the "mighty change" in their hearts (Alma 5.) It might be said that this is the initial stage of receiving "His seal in their foreheads." Inasmuch as we are followers of Christ, we belong to Him. He is our master and we are His disciples. Using the same imagery, we see that those who follow the principles of Satan's kingdom carry his mark of ownership. Alma 34: 35 makes very clear the fact that "if ye have procrastinated the day of [our] repentance even until death, behold, ye are subjected to the spirit of the devil, and he doth seal you his." These become eligible for this mark (or seal) by worshipping the beast and also the image of the beast. (See vss. 4, 15.) Worshipping the beast is following after the things of this world. It is disobeying God, and refusing to repent. Verse 17 indicates that this mark determined who could "buy or sell." Satan has elsewhere claimed his philosophy that everything could be attained through money. He has also threatened to obtain the treasures of the earth and buy up Popes and armies. Obviously, materialism is one of Satan's most potent weapons. As some have speculated, the mark could in some way be connected to economic, political or religious allegiances. As is the case throughout this book, the specific fulfillment could come in a myriad of ways, but most important is what the mark signifies--Who is our master.

The stamping of an arm or forehead was a common method of marking slaves in John's day. Notice the parallel of the beast's marking with the Lord's. Again, a case of imitation. See Revelation 7: 2-3 where the Lord delays the four destroying angels until he has had time to seal "the servants of our God in their

The Image and Mark of the Beast (15-18) 1.He had power to give life unto the image of the beast (15) 2.That the image of the beast should both speak (15) 3.And cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed (15) As mentioned earlier in these notes, the beast is not to be understood as the "Great and Abominable Church." The "Great and Abominable Church" is the Harlot who rides upon the beast in chapter 17. Often saints get these two confused. The mark of the beast does not identify the harlot, but those who worship the beast. Of course, both the beast and the harlot belong to Satan's kingdom. 4.He causeth all.… to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads (16) "As the servants of God have their calling and election made sure when they are 'sealed … in their

foreheads' (Rev. 7: 2-8), so the Great Imitator places a mark in the right hand or foreheads of those who follow him. Figuratively, this means they receive blessings -- if his rewards lawfully may be so named -from under his hands. In the literal sense, we have, of course, seen marks and signs which identify and set apart those who adhere to religious systems that are not of God and which use economic sanctions as a tool to force adherence to their system of worship. It will be interesting -- interesting? nay, fascinating! -- to see what the future holds as the full meaning of this passage unfolds and the identity of the actual 'beast' is revealed." [Bruce R. Mc Conkie, DNTC 3: 524.]

quotes.text: Could it be true that if we put our trust in stocks, bonds, money, armaments, etc. that we are worshipping false gods? And do we give these material things power over us? Can we be in bondage through debt and selfishness? If so, who really is our master? Does this slavery ultimately give Satan power to dictate who might buy or sell?
quotes.speaker: Spencer W. Kimball
quotes.attr: Spencer W. Kimball, "False Gods We Worship," June 1976 Ensign

notes: In order to keep before his readers the eventual reward of their endurance, John intersperses glimpses of final glory and joy among his presentations of judgment. The detailed description of the sea beast and the land beast could cause hopel fear and dash hope if not balanced with a reminder of ultimate victory. A note of encouragement is in order. John moves quickly beyond the storm to the bright morning of triumph, when the Lamb and his followers stand on the heavenly Zion with the song of redemption resounding from the faithful.

An important concept here is that the fact the brethren will lead us through these times if we look to them. Follow their counsel. When or if the specific beast needs to be identified for our generation then it will be by the Lord through his servants.

quotes.text: A possibility is that the number is symbolic. The number seven indicates perfection; three sevens would suggest perfection to an emphatic level. Three sixes suggest a marked falling short of perfection. Seven could be a symbolic number for Christ; six could indicate the anti-Christ. [many early Greek manuscripts of the book of Revelation give the number as 616]
quotes.source: Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co.
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 176-177
